description: Enriched process catalog. AUTHORITATIVE for per-process definitions, citations, category placement, and editorial notes, and for per-category scope / out-of-scope / issues / history. The `tasks[]` and `task_count` fields inside each process are derived from task_details.json by a separate sync script (sync_process_details_tasks.py) and should not be hand-edited.
source_script: consolidation: session_2026-04-19 (reconciled with process_reference.md and process_categories.md)

categories.name: Associative Learning and Reinforcement
categories.scope: Learning of stimulus-stimulus, stimulus-response, and action-outcome contingencies under reinforcement; Pavlovian and instrumental conditioning; extinction; reversal; model-based and model-free reinforcement learning; reward prediction error.
categories.out_of_scope: Structure learning without scalar reinforcement (that is Implicit and Statistical Learning).
categories.issues: Instrumental ≡ Operant conditioning resolved 2026-04-18: collapsed to single `hed_instrumental_conditioning` entry. Operant conditioning absorbed as synonym.

categories.name: Auditory and Pre-Attentive Deviance Processing
categories.scope: Pitch perception, auditory tone discrimination, acoustic processing, auditory perception; the family of processes indexed by MMN / oddball paradigms.
categories.issues: Tightly coupled to a specific measurement tradition (ERP). If the catalog ever generalizes beyond MMN, the category boundary with Selective and Sustained Attention (feature-based attention) will need review.

categories.name: Awareness, Agency, and Metacognition
categories.scope: Conscious experience of perception (perceptual awareness, masking); metacognitive monitoring and control; sense of agency; body ownership; interoceptive awareness; self-referential processing; feeling of knowing; judgment of learning; mind wandering; self-monitoring; attentional awareness.
categories.out_of_scope: "Consciousness" as a trait or umbrella construct.
categories.issues: The scope boundary with Selective and Sustained Attention is thin (attentional awareness vs. attention itself). Keep the test: a process goes here if the reportable content is *what the participant consciously experiences*, not the selection mechanism producing it.
categories.history: This category absorbed the dissolved "Memory Control and Metamemory" entries (feeling of knowing, judgment of learning) and the Self-monitoring row from Inhibitory Control; perceptual-awareness rows from the pre-reframe "Perceptual Awareness and Consciousness" category; and six 2026-04-15 additions (metacognitive monitoring, metacognitive control, sense of agency, body ownership, interoceptive awareness, self-referential processing).

categories.name: Cognitive Flexibility and Higher-Order Executive Function
categories.scope: Task-set reconfiguration (set shifting), rule updating, strategy selection, goal maintenance. Current inhabitants: goal maintenance, set shifting, strategy use.
categories.out_of_scope: Fluid intelligence (an individual-difference construct, not a process); working-memory updating (in Short-Term and Working Memory); "cognitive flexibility" as a capacity-level umbrella (dropped 2026-04-19; alias on `hed_set_shifting`).
categories.issues: The user has earlier flagged that **attentional flexibility** and **mental flexibility** should perhaps be separate sub-processes — deferred.
categories.history: `hed_cognitive_flexibility` dropped 2026-04-19 — broader construct (capacity-level) that fails the single-answer inclusion test; set shifting is its primary operationalization. References absorbed into `hed_set_shifting`; 'Cognitive flexibility' added as alias.

categories.name: Emotion Perception and Regulation
categories.scope: Recognition of emotional signals (face, voice, body), affective priming, cognitive reappraisal, expressive suppression, emotion regulation strategies.
categories.out_of_scope: Specific emotions as states (fear, happiness, disgust, anger, sadness — HED handles these as stimulus/state labels); affect as a trait; mood.
categories.issues: This category had heavy non-process pollution in the pre-reframe list (16 of 21 entries were specific emotions, valence, arousal, mood, or empathy-as-trait). Current 5-row count reflects the cleaned process-only version. Coverage of appraisal-stage processes is thin — expected to grow if we add paradigms that specifically target appraisal.

categories.name: Face and Object Perception
categories.scope: Processes that recover identity, category, or structure from sensory form: face identity, face perception, visual object recognition, visual form recognition, visual perception, pattern recognition, motion perception (including biological motion), depth perception; plus the non-visual object-perception rows (somatosensory, gustatory, olfactory, proprioceptive) that have no better home.
categories.out_of_scope: Faces and objects as *stimulus categories* — HED handles those on the stimulus axis.
categories.issues: The non-visual perceptual rows (gustatory, olfactory, somatosensory) are grouped here pragmatically rather than because they belong with faces and objects. If a separate "Non-visual perception" category is ever needed, it would pull them out. For now the grouping reflects "perception of form across modalities".

categories.name: Implicit and Statistical Learning
categories.scope: Acquisition of structure without explicit instruction: implicit memory, procedural memory. Paradigm-level coverage includes artificial grammar learning, serial reaction time, statistical word-segmentation, perceptual category learning — these live in `task_details.json`, not as separate process rows.
categories.out_of_scope: Reinforcement-driven associative learning (Associative Learning and Reinforcement).
categories.issues: The category is intentionally lean at the process level because most of the work happens at the task level. If sequence-structure learning becomes distinct enough to need its own process row, it should live here.

categories.name: Inhibitory Control and Conflict Monitoring
categories.scope: Response inhibition, response conflict, conflict monitoring, interference control, executive attention, proactive control, reactive control, error detection, error correction.
categories.out_of_scope: Impulsivity as a trait; behavioral inhibition as a temperament construct.
categories.issues: Self-monitoring was moved out to Awareness, Agency, and Metacognition during the reframe. The proactive/reactive control split is paradigm-bound (AX-CPT-style designs); retained but worth revisiting if the catalog adds paradigms that dissociate other control modes.

categories.name: Language Comprehension and Production
categories.scope: Mapping sound or text to meaning and meaning to articulation: lexical access, word recognition, reading, phonological processes, syntactic parsing, sentence comprehension, discourse processing, semantic processing, semantic knowledge, naming, verbal fluency, speech perception, speech production, language comprehension, language production.
categories.out_of_scope: "Language" as a faculty; linguistic representations themselves; motor aspects of articulation (those live in Motor Preparation, Timing, and Execution as vocal-motor control, with a pointer from here).
categories.issues: Visual word recognition ≡ word recognition — merged during the reframe (canonical: `hed_word_recognition`). Reading as a process vs. reading as a behavior is still somewhat conflated in `hed_reading` — an open question.

categories.name: Long-Term Memory
categories.scope: Encoding, consolidation, retrieval; recall and recognition; recollection and familiarity; source memory; episodic, semantic, autobiographical, verbal memory; pattern completion and separation; proactive and retroactive interference; forgetting; directed forgetting; reconsolidation; prospective memory.
categories.out_of_scope: "Memory" as an umbrella; semantic representation itself (that lives closer to Language as semantic knowledge); short-term storage (Short-Term and Working Memory).
categories.issues: The recollection / familiarity / source-memory sub-distinctions are preserved as separate rows. Further sub-splitting (e.g., recollection by domain) is deferred.
categories.history: Inherited Directed forgetting from the dissolved "Memory Control and Metamemory" category.

categories.name: Motor Preparation, Timing, and Execution
categories.scope: Motor planning, response selection, motor preparation, action initiation, response execution, motor timing, motor sequence learning, motor memory, visuomotor adaptation, fine motor control, reaching, grasping, saccade, antisaccade, proprioception, vocal-motor control.
categories.out_of_scope: Motor traits (handedness, dexterity); motor umbrella concepts.
categories.issues: Antisaccade is currently filed here; it could be argued into Inhibitory Control (since it centrally depends on prepotent-saccade inhibition). Retained here because it is an oculomotor output measure. Proprioception is the only pure-perception row here; if it ever moves out it would go to Face and Object Perception's non-visual pocket.

categories.name: Perceptual Decision-Making (Evidence Accumulation)
categories.scope: Integration of noisy sensory evidence to a decision criterion.
categories.issues: Singleton row. Overlaps with Perceptual Awareness on confidence and with Value-Based Decision-Making on accumulation-model machinery — but the evidence source (perceptual vs. value) is the category boundary. If the catalog ever adds distinct rows for decision boundary, drift rate, confidence formation, and speed-accuracy tradeoff as processes (rather than task-level parameters), they belong here.

categories.name: Reasoning and Problem-Solving
categories.scope: Deductive, inductive, causal, analogical, mathematical reasoning; hypothesis testing; categorization; insight; means-ends analysis; planning; subgoaling.
categories.out_of_scope: "Reasoning" as umbrella; "logical reasoning" as umbrella over deductive + inductive; fluid intelligence as an individual-difference construct.
categories.history: Four problem-solving rows added during 2026-04-15: Insight, Means-ends analysis, Planning, Subgoaling. (`hed_planning` had been accidentally duplicated in `process_list.md` and was de-duplicated on 2026-04-17.) `hed_logical_reasoning` dropped 2026-04-19 — umbrella over deductive + inductive; alias added to `hed_deductive_reasoning`.

categories.name: Reward Anticipation and Motivation
categories.scope: Anticipation of reward, consumption of reward, incentive salience, effort allocation, approach motivation, avoidance motivation.
categories.out_of_scope: Motivation as a trait; reward sensitivity as an individual difference.
categories.history: The appetitive/aversive axis pair was collapsed during 2026-04-15 (dropped as stimulus properties rather than processes); Approach and Avoidance motivation are retained.

categories.name: Selective and Sustained Attention
categories.scope: Attention networks (alerting, orienting, executive attention); selective attention; sustained attention; attention shifting; attentional capture; divided attention; feature-based attention; object-based attention; spatial attention; temporal attention.
categories.out_of_scope: Attention as an umbrella; alertness as a trait; arousal as a physiological state.
categories.issues: Sustained attention ≡ vigilance when operationalized as a continuous-performance measure; treated as synonymous in the current row definition. If vigilance-as-trait ever needs representation, it would live outside this category.

categories.name: Short-Term and Working Memory
categories.scope: Active maintenance, manipulation, working-memory updating, rehearsal, chunking; visual working memory; verbal working memory; spatial working memory; umbrella `hed_working_memory`.
categories.out_of_scope: Working memory **load** (task parameter, not process); working memory **capacity** (individual difference); long-term memory; bare "maintenance" (merged into `hed_active_maintenance`).
categories.issues: Working-memory SPLIT (umbrella item 3 from the earlier decisions log) still pending — whether `hed_working_memory` should be retained as an umbrella alongside the component rows, or whether pages should link only to the components.
categories.history: The duplicate `hed_updating` / `hed_updating_wm` pair (pre-reframe residue from when Updating was split across the executive-function and working-memory categories) was consolidated on 2026-04-17 into a single `hed_working_memory_updating` row. The renaming also improves specificity — plain "Updating" was memory-context-underspecified. See `.status/working_memory_updating_rename_2026-04-17.md`. `hed_maintenance` merged into `hed_active_maintenance` 2026-04-19 — "active maintenance" is the preferred term; Goldman-Rakic (1995) reference absorbed; "Maintenance" added as alias.

categories.name: Social Cognition and Strategic Social Choice
categories.scope: Theory of mind (canonical: `hed_perspective_taking`; `hed_mentalizing` was merged during the reframe), self-other distinction, joint attention, imitation, in-group/out-group processing, stereotyping, social decision-making, social perception, cooperation, competition, reciprocity.
categories.out_of_scope: Empathy as a trait; social anxiety; "social cognition" as umbrella.
categories.issues: Trust as a process (vs. trust as a trait) was discussed during the reframe but no dedicated row was added; if a Trust Game process row becomes warranted, it fits here.

categories.name: Spatial Cognition and Navigation
categories.issues: Very small at the process level because most spatial cognition is paradigm-specific (handled at the task level in `task_details.json`). If allocentric/egocentric representation, path integration, or boundary cell processing ever need process-level rows, they go here.

categories.name: Value-Based Decision-Making Under Risk and Uncertainty
categories.scope: Choice among options that differ in subjective value: valuation, value-based decision making, choice commitment, delay discounting, intertemporal choice, probability judgment, risk processing.
categories.history: Two valuation rows added during 2026-04-15: Valuation, Choice commitment. A duplicate `Value-based decision making` tail entry introduced by an earlier aborted edit was removed from `process_reference.md` on 2026-04-17.

processes.definition: Learning of co-occurrence relations between stimuli or between stimuli and responses.
Decrease in a previously reinforced response when reinforcement is withheld; a form of new inhibitory learning rather than erasure.
Behavior that is sensitive to current outcome value, characteristic of action–outcome learning.
Behavior that is insensitive to the current value of its outcome, characteristic of stimulus–response learning.

aliases.name: Operant conditioning
aliases.note: Skinnerian terminology; emphasizes the operant response and reinforcement schedules. Merged from separate entry 2026-04-18.

processes.definition: Learning that an action produces an outcome; also called operant conditioning. Encompasses both goal-directed (action–outcome) and habitual (stimulus–response) control, studied via reinforcement schedules and outcome-devaluation procedures.

recent_references.citation_string: Dickinson & Balleine (2002) in *Stevens' Handbook of Experimental Psychology*

processes.definition: Reinforcement learning that uses an internal model of the environment's transition and reward structure to plan.
Reinforcement learning from cached value estimates updated by prediction errors, without an explicit model of the environment.
Learning that a neutral stimulus predicts a biologically significant outcome, leading to conditioned responding.
Direct learning of a mapping from states to actions without necessarily estimating values.

recent_references.citation_string: Sutton & Barto (2018) *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction*, 2nd ed

processes.definition: Learning to select actions that maximize cumulative reward through experience with reward prediction errors.
Relearning after contingencies between stimuli (or responses) and outcomes are switched.
Signed difference between received and expected reward, instantiated by phasic midbrain dopamine firing.
Acquisition of the expected value of stimuli, actions, or states from experience with outcomes.
Low-level analysis of sound, including frequency, intensity, and temporal structure.
Perception of sound, including pitch, timbre, loudness, and spatial location.
Judgment of whether two tones differ in pitch, intensity, or duration.

recent_references.journal: An Introduction to the Psychology of Hearing
recent_references.citation_string: Moore (2012) *An Introduction to the Psychology of Hearing*, 6th ed

processes.definition: Perception of the highness or lowness of a sound, related to fundamental frequency.
Awareness of the current focus and content of attention.

The experience that a body or body-part belongs to the self, revealed by multisensory illusions in which synchronous visuo-tactile or visuo-proprioceptive input shifts the perceived location of the body.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Ehrsson HH, Spence C & Passingham RE (2004) *Science* 305:875-877

recent_references.citation_string: Tsakiris M (2017) *Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology* 70:597-609
Blanke O, Slater M & Serino A (2015) *Neuron* 88:145-166

processes.definition: Judgment that information currently not retrievable would be recognized if presented.

recent_references.citation_string: Schwartz & Cleary (2016) in *The Oxford Handbook of Metamemory*

processes.definition: Conscious perception of internal bodily signals such as heartbeat, respiration, and visceral state; indexed by heartbeat-detection accuracy, heartbeat-evoked potentials, and interoceptive sensibility measures.

recent_references.citation_string: Khalsa SS et al. (2018) *Biological Psychiatry: CNNI* 3:501-513
Critchley HD & Garfinkel SN (2017) *Current Opinion in Psychology* 17:7-14

processes.definition: Prediction of future memory performance made during or after encoding.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior
fundamental_references.citation_string: Arbuckle & Cuddy (1969) *Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior* 8:126–131

processes.definition: Reduction in visibility or detectability of a target stimulus by a temporally or spatially adjacent masker.

recent_references.citation_string: Enns & Di Lollo (2000) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 4:345–352

processes.definition: Regulation of cognition based on metacognitive monitoring - study-time allocation, strategy selection, answer withholding, and restart decisions - linking second-order evaluation to first-order behavior.

fundamental_references.journal: The Psychology of Learning and Motivation
fundamental_references.citation_string: Nelson TO & Narens L (1990) *The Psychology of Learning and Motivation* 26:125-173
Son LK & Metcalfe J (2000) *Journal of Experimental Psychology: LMC* 26:204-221

recent_references.citation_string: Ackerman R & Thompson VA (2017) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 21:607-617
Desender K, Boldt A & Yeung N (2018) *Psychological Science* 29:761-778

processes.definition: Second-order evaluation of ongoing first-order cognition - how confident one is in a perception, memory, or judgment - yielding reportable assessments that dissociate from first-order accuracy.

fundamental_references.journal: The Psychology of Learning and Motivation
fundamental_references.citation_string: Nelson TO & Narens L (1990) *The Psychology of Learning and Motivation* 26:125-173
Koriat A (1997) *Journal of Experimental Psychology: General* 126:349-370

recent_references.citation_string: Fleming SM & Lau HC (2014) *Frontiers in Human Neuroscience* 8:443
Maniscalco B & Lau H (2012) *Consciousness and Cognition* 21:422-430

processes.definition: Task-unrelated thought that arises during an ongoing task.

recent_references.citation_string: Christoff, Irving, Fox, Spreng & Andrews-Hanna (2016) *Nature Reviews Neuroscience* 17:718–731

processes.definition: Conscious access to perceptual content.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Dehaene, Changeux, Naccache, Sackur & Sergent (2006) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 10:204–211

processes.definition: Ongoing evaluation of one's own performance against task goals and expected outcomes.

fundamental_references.journal: The Psychology of Learning and Motivation
fundamental_references.citation_string: Nelson & Narens (1990) in *The Psychology of Learning and Motivation*

processes.definition: Processing of information in relation to the self, yielding enhanced encoding for self-relevant material and characteristic engagement of cortical midline structures (mPFC, PCC).

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
fundamental_references.citation_string: Rogers TB, Kuiper NA & Kirker WS (1977) *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology* 35:677-688
Kelley WM et al. (2002) *Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience* 14:785-794

recent_references.citation_string: Murray RJ, Schaer M & Debbané M (2012) *Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews* 36:1043-1059

processes.definition: The experience of being the cause of one's own actions and their sensory consequences, dissociable into implicit (intentional binding) and explicit (authorship judgment) components.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Haggard P, Clark S & Kalogeras J (2002) *Nature Neuroscience* 5:382-385

recent_references.citation_string: Moore JW & Obhi SS (2012) *Consciousness and Cognition* 21:546-561

processes.definition: Active holding of task goals, rules, or sub-goals in a form that biases ongoing processing.

Flexible reconfiguration of the task set in response to a change in task demands; the primary experimental operationalization of cognitive flexibility, measured by task-switching paradigms and the WCST.

aliases.name: Cognitive flexibility
aliases.note: Broader construct encompassing set shifting, perspective-taking, and adaptive strategy use; dropped as separate process 2026-04-19 because it fails the single-answer inclusion test. Set shifting is the primary experimental operationalization.

recent_references.citation_string: Kiesel, Steinhauser, Wendt, Falkenstein, Jost, Philipp & Koch (2010) *Psychological Bulletin* 136:849–874

processes.definition: Selection and implementation of a cognitive procedure chosen to improve performance on a task.
Facilitation or interference in evaluating a target by a valence-related prime.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
fundamental_references.citation_string: Fazio, Sanbonmatsu, Powell & Kardes (1986) *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology* 50:229–238

recent_references.citation_string: Herring, White, Jabeen, Hinojos, Terrell, Reyes, Schubert & Crites (2013) *Psychological Bulletin* 139:1062–1089

processes.definition: Reinterpretation of an emotional stimulus to change its affective impact.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
fundamental_references.citation_string: Gross (1998) *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology* 74:224–237

recent_references.citation_string: Buhle, Silvers, Wager, Lopez, Onyemekwu, Kober, Weber & Ochsner (2014) *Cerebral Cortex* 24:2981–2990

aliases.name: Emotion perception
aliases.note: Broader term; recognition is the operationalized form (identification of a named emotion category).

processes.definition: Identification of emotional states from cues such as facial expression or vocal prosody.

tasks.canonical_name: Reading the Mind in the Eyes Task

processes.definition: Processes by which individuals influence which emotions they have, when, and how they experience and express them.
Inhibition of outward behavioral expression of emotion.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
fundamental_references.citation_string: Gross & Levenson (1993) *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology* 64:970–986

processes.definition: Recognition of animate motion from sparse kinematic cues, such as point-light displays.
Perception of distance and three-dimensional structure from monocular and binocular cues.
Identification of an individual from face-specific features.

recent_references.citation_string: Duchaine & Yovel (2015) *Annual Review of Vision Science* 1:393–416

processes.definition: Recognition and processing of faces as a specialized perceptual category; N170-sensitive, FFA-dependent.

tasks.canonical_name: Reading the Mind in the Eyes Task

aliases.name: Gustation
aliases.note: Pre-reframe name; renamed 2026-04-15 for consistency with other modality-perception rows.

processes.definition: Perception of taste qualities (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami).
Detection and interpretation of moving stimuli; includes global motion and optic flow.
Perception of odor via the olfactory system.
Categorization of sensory input according to its structure.

fundamental_references.journal: Symposium on the Mechanisation of Thought Processes
fundamental_references.citation_string: Selfridge (1959) in *Symposium on the Mechanisation of Thought Processes*

aliases.name: Somatosensation
aliases.note: Pre-reframe name; renamed 2026-04-15 for consistency with other modality-perception rows.

processes.definition: Perception of bodily sensations, including touch, proprioception, temperature, and pain.
Assignment of visual input to a shape category prior to semantic identification.
Identification of objects from visual input, invariant over viewpoint, size, and lighting.
Perception of visual information; includes form, motion, depth, color, and spatial layout.

recent_references.citation_string: Kravitz, Saleem, Baker, Ungerleider & Mishkin (2013) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 17:26–49

processes.definition: Memory expressed without conscious recollection, measurable by priming, skill learning, and conditioning.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Graf & Schacter (1985) *JEP: Learning, Memory, and Cognition* 11:501–518

recent_references.citation_string: Schacter, Wig & Stevens (2007) *Current Opinion in Neurobiology* 17:171–176

processes.definition: Non-declarative memory for skills and procedures, expressed through improved performance rather than conscious recollection.

recent_references.citation_string: Doyon, Gabitov, Vahdat, Lungu & Boutin (2018) *Neurobiology of Learning and Memory* 153:1–12

processes.definition: Detection of co-activation of incompatible response tendencies, proposed to recruit anterior cingulate cortex and trigger cognitive control.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Botvinick, Braver, Barch, Carter & Cohen (2001) *Psychological Review* 108:624–652

processes.definition: Adjustment of behavior following an error, including post-error slowing and improvement on subsequent trials.
Recognition that a response was incorrect, indexed by the error-related negativity (ERN) and by error awareness.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Falkenstein, Hohnsbein, Hoormann & Blanke (1991) *EEG & Clinical Neurophysiology* 78:447–455
Gehring, Goss, Coles, Meyer & Donchin (1993) *Psychological Science* 4:385–390

processes.definition: Resolution of conflict among thoughts, feelings, and responses; one of Posner's three attentional networks.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Fan, McCandliss, Sommer, Raz & Posner (2002) *Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience* 14:340–347

processes.definition: Resistance to interference from task-irrelevant stimuli or competing response representations, measured by Stroop, Flanker, and Simon effects.

recent_references.journal: Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry
recent_references.citation_string: Nigg (2017) *Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry* 58:361–383

processes.definition: Sustained, anticipatory maintenance of task goals that biases processing in preparation for an expected demand.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Braver, Gray & Burgess (2007) in *Variation in Working Memory*

processes.definition: Transient, stimulus-triggered engagement of control after interference or conflict is detected.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Braver, Gray & Burgess (2007) in *Variation in Working Memory*

processes.definition: Competing activation of two or more response representations on a single trial, typically engaging cognitive control.
Suppression of a prepotent or already-initiated response when it becomes inappropriate, indexed behaviorally by stop-signal reaction time or commission errors.

recent_references.citation_string: Aron, Robbins & Poldrack (2014) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 18:177–185

processes.definition: Integration of sentences into coherent representations of extended text or conversation.

recent_references.citation_string: Ferstl, Neumann, Bogler & von Cramon (2008) *Human Brain Mapping* 29:581–593

processes.definition: Extraction of meaning from linguistic input (spoken, written, or signed).
Generation of spoken, written, or signed linguistic output, from message to articulation.
Retrieval of word-level representations (form and meaning) from memory.
Production of a word label for a presented stimulus (e.g., picture naming).

fundamental_references.citation_string: Oldfield & Wingfield (1965) *Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology* 17:273–281

processes.definition: Explicit awareness of the sound structure of spoken words (onsets, rimes, phonemes).

fundamental_references.citation_string: Liberman, Shankweiler, Fischer & Carter (1974) *Journal of Experimental Child Psychology* 18:201–212

processes.definition: Assembly of phonological representations during language production.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Levelt, Roelofs & Meyer (1999) *Behavioral and Brain Sciences* 22:1–75

processes.definition: Visual processing of written text, integrating orthography, phonology, and meaning.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Coltheart, Rastle, Perry, Langdon & Ziegler (2001) *Psychological Review* 108:204–256

recent_references.citation_string: Dehaene, Cohen, Morais & Kolinsky (2015) *Nature Reviews Neuroscience* 16:234–244

processes.definition: Long-term store of facts, concepts, and word meanings.

recent_references.citation_string: Ralph, Jefferies, Patterson & Rogers (2017) *Nature Reviews Neuroscience* 18:42–55

processes.definition: Access and integration of word and phrase meaning, indexed by the N400.
Integration of lexical, syntactic, and semantic information to derive sentence meaning.
Extraction of linguistic content from the acoustic speech signal, including phoneme categorization, word segmentation, and prosodic processing.

recent_references.citation_string: Diehl, Lotto & Holt (2004) *Annual Review of Psychology* 55:149–179

processes.definition: Planning and articulation of spoken output; the motor component of articulation is covered under vocal-motor control in Motor Preparation, Timing, and Execution.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Levelt, Roelofs & Meyer (1999) *Behavioral and Brain Sciences* 22:1–75

processes.definition: Assignment of hierarchical grammatical structure to a linguistic input.

recent_references.journal: Automaton Theories of Human Sentence Comprehension
recent_references.citation_string: Hale (2014) *Automaton Theories of Human Sentence Comprehension*

processes.definition: Rapid generation of words under a semantic or phonemic constraint.

recent_references.citation_string: Shao, Janse, Visser & Meyer (2014) *Frontiers in Psychology* 5:772

aliases.name: Visual word recognition
aliases.note: Visual is the default modality in cognitive psychology; auditory word recognition has its own name (spoken-word recognition).

processes.definition: Identification of a word as a lexical item from its perceptual input.
Memory for personally experienced events across the lifespan, integrating episodic and semantic components.
Post-encoding stabilization of memory traces, dependent on time and often on sleep; includes synaptic and systems consolidation.
Consciously accessible memory for facts and events; encompasses semantic and episodic memory.
Reduced memory for items that have been cued to be forgotten, relative to items cued to be remembered.
Processes by which perceptual input is transformed into a memory representation at acquisition.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior
fundamental_references.citation_string: Craik & Lockhart (1972) *Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior* 11:671–684

processes.definition: Memory for specific events located in a particular place and time and accompanied by autonoetic consciousness.

recent_references.citation_string: Moscovitch, Cabeza, Winocur & Nadel (2016) *Annual Review of Psychology* 67:105–134

processes.definition: Sense that a stimulus has been encountered before, in the absence of retrieval of contextual detail.
Loss of accessibility of previously encoded information, due to decay, interference, or retrieval failure.

recent_references.citation_string: Hardt, Nader & Nadel (2013) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 17:111–120

processes.definition: Retrieval of a complete memory from a partial or degraded cue; CA3 function.
Transformation of similar input patterns into distinct, non-overlapping memory representations; dentate gyrus function.
Disruption of new learning by previously learned material.
Memory for intentions to act at a future time or on a future event.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Einstein & McDaniel (1990) *JEP: Learning, Memory, and Cognition* 16:717–726

recent_references.journal: Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition
recent_references.citation_string: McDaniel, Umanath, Einstein & Waldum (2015) *Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition* 4:268–279

processes.definition: Retrieval of items without an external cue provided at test (free or cued recall).
Judgment that a test item has been previously encountered; supported by familiarity and recollection.
Retrieval of contextual detail about a prior event, including source information.

recent_references.citation_string: Diana, Yonelinas & Ranganath (2007) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 11:379–386

processes.definition: Destabilization and re-stabilization of a memory upon retrieval, creating a window for modification.
Reactivation of a stored memory representation; dissociable into cue-driven and strategic retrieval.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior
fundamental_references.citation_string: Tulving & Pearlstone (1966) *Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior* 5:381–391

processes.definition: Disruption of older memories by newly learned material.
Long-term store of general knowledge about the world — facts, concepts, and word meanings — dissociable from episodic memory and supported by distributed cortical representations.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior
fundamental_references.citation_string: Collins & Quillian (1969) *Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior* 8:240–247

processes.definition: Memory for the contextual origin of information (e.g., who said it, where it was seen).
Memory for linguistic material (words, sentences), tested via word lists, story recall, and RAVLT.

recent_references.journal: Rey Auditory and Verbal Learning Test
recent_references.citation_string: Schmidt (1996) *Rey Auditory and Verbal Learning Test*

processes.definition: Triggering of an action after planning is complete.
Voluntary eye movement away from a peripheral stimulus, requiring inhibition of a prepotent saccade.
Precise control of small-amplitude movements, typically of the hand and fingers.

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery
fundamental_references.citation_string: Napier (1956) *Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery* 38B:902–913

processes.definition: Shaping and closure of the hand around an object.
Long-term retention of motor skills and procedures.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Karni, Meyer, Rey-Hipolito, Jezzard, Adams, Turner & Ungerleider (1998) *PNAS* 95:861–868

recent_references.citation_string: Krakauer, Hadjiosif, Xu, Wong & Haith (2019) *Comprehensive Physiology* 9:613–663

processes.definition: Specification of movement parameters before execution.
Neural readiness state preceding movement, indexed by the readiness potential and LRP.
Acquisition of a skilled sequence of movements through repetition.

recent_references.citation_string: Doyon, Bellec, Amsel, Penhune, Monchi, Carrier, Lehéricy & Benali (2009) *Behavioural Brain Research* 199:61–75

processes.definition: Production or estimation of temporal intervals in motor output.

recent_references.citation_string: Merchant, Harrington & Meck (2013) *Annual Review of Neuroscience* 36:313–336

processes.definition: Perception of body position and movement from muscle, tendon, and joint receptors.

fundamental_references.journal: The Integrative Action of the Nervous System
fundamental_references.citation_string: Sherrington (1906) *The Integrative Action of the Nervous System*

processes.definition: Goal-directed arm movement toward a spatial target.

fundamental_references.journal: The Neural and Behavioural Organization of Goal-Directed Movements
fundamental_references.citation_string: Jeannerod (1988) *The Neural and Behavioural Organization of Goal-Directed Movements*

recent_references.citation_string: Shadmehr, Smith & Krakauer (2010) *Annual Review of Neuroscience* 33:89–108

processes.definition: Overt production of a selected response in accordance with task demands.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Sternberg, Monsell, Knoll & Wright (1978) in *Information Processing in Motor Control*

recent_references.citation_string: Haith & Krakauer (2013) in *Progress in Motor Control*

processes.definition: Choice of an action from a set of alternatives.
Rapid ballistic eye movement between points of fixation.
Recalibration of movement in response to perturbations of the mapping between vision and action.

recent_references.citation_string: Krakauer, Hadjiosif, Xu, Wong & Haith (2019) *Comprehensive Physiology* 9:613–663

processes.definition: Control of the articulators for speech production and vocalization.
Integration of noisy sensory evidence toward a decision criterion, modeled by sequential-sampling frameworks (drift-diffusion, race models) and indexed by accuracy and response-time distributions.
Mapping of relational structure from a source domain to a target domain.

recent_references.journal: Oxford Handbook of Thinking and Reasoning
recent_references.citation_string: Holyoak (2012) *Oxford Handbook of Thinking and Reasoning*, ch 14

processes.definition: Assignment of instances to categories according to their features.
Inference about cause–effect relations from observation and intervention.

aliases.name: Logical reasoning
aliases.note: Broader term encompassing deductive and inductive forms; dropped as separate entry 2026-04-18 because its definition was explicitly a union of the two. Wason Selection Task link reassigned here.

processes.definition: Inference in which the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises.
Generation and evaluation of alternative explanations against evidence.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Bruner, Goodnow & Austin (1956) *A Study of Thinking*

processes.definition: Inference from specific instances to general principles; probabilistic rather than necessary.
Sudden restructuring of a problem representation yielding a solution that was previously unavailable, often accompanied by an "Aha!" experience distinct from incremental analytic search.

recent_references.citation_string: Bowden EM & Jung-Beeman M (2003) *Psychonomic Bulletin & Review* 10:730-737
Kounios J & Beeman M (2014) *Annual Review of Psychology* 65:71-93

processes.definition: Manipulation of numerical and algebraic quantities and relations.
Iterative problem-solving strategy in which the distance between the current state and the goal state is reduced by selecting operators that diminish the largest remaining difference.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Newell A & Simon HA (1972) *Human Problem Solving* Prentice-Hall

recent_references.citation_string: Huys QJM et al. (2015) *PLoS Computational Biology* 11:e1004112

processes.definition: Mental formulation of a sequence of actions toward a goal before execution.
Decomposition of a goal into an ordered sequence of subgoals whose satisfaction advances the overall task; dissociable from means-ends search and often studied through hierarchical task analysis.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Egan DE & Greeno JG (1974) in *Knowledge and Cognition* Erlbaum
Anderson JR (1983) *The Architecture of Cognition* Harvard University Press

processes.definition: Tendency to engage with stimuli associated with positive outcomes.
Tendency to disengage from stimuli associated with negative outcomes.
Decision to expend physical or cognitive effort as a function of expected reward and cost.
"Wanting" attributed to reward-predictive cues, distinct from "liking."
Affective and neural response to cues predicting upcoming reward.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Knutson, Adams, Fong & Hommer (2001) *Journal of Neuroscience* 21:RC159

recent_references.citation_string: Oldham, Murawski, Fornito, Youssef, Yücel & Lorenzetti (2018) *Human Brain Mapping* 39:3398–3418

processes.definition: Affective and neural response to receipt of reward.
Achieving and maintaining a state of readiness to respond, whether tonic or phasic; one of Posner's three attentional networks.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Fan, McCandliss, Sommer, Raz & Posner (2002) *Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience* 14:340–347

processes.definition: Reorienting of attention from one location, feature, or object to another, indexed by cost in RT or accuracy.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Posner, Walker, Friedrich & Rafal (1984) *Journal of Neuroscience* 4:1863–1874

processes.definition: Involuntary shift of attention to a salient stimulus that is not the current target of task-directed attention.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Yantis & Jonides (1984) *JEP: Human Perception and Performance* 10:601–621

processes.definition: Concurrent processing of two or more streams of information or two tasks, typically yielding dual-task interference.
Selection of a specific visual feature (e.g., a color, orientation, or motion direction) that enhances processing of that feature throughout the visual field.
Selection of an object as a unit of attention such that the attended features belonging to the object are processed together.
Selection of information from sensory input, typically in space, by covert or overt shifts of attention; one of Posner's three attentional networks.
Prioritized processing of a task-relevant subset of stimuli (a location, feature, object, or modality) with suppression of irrelevant stimuli.

recent_references.citation_string: Chun, Golomb & Turk-Browne (2011) *Annual Review of Psychology* 62:73–101

processes.definition: Selection of a location in space for preferential sensory processing, measurable via cueing benefits and N2pc/P1 modulation.

aliases.name: Vigilance
aliases.note: Synonymous when operationalized as a continuous-performance measure.

processes.definition: Maintenance of attentional engagement over extended intervals during which targets are rare or responses are repetitive; synonymous with vigilance.
Selection of a point in time for preferential processing, measurable via cueing benefits in time and the attentional blink.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Raymond, Shapiro & Arnell (1992) *JEP: Human Perception and Performance* 18:849–860

aliases.name: Maintenance
aliases.note: Generic term for holding information over a delay; merged 2026-04-18. Active maintenance emphasizes the volitional, attention-demanding character.

processes.definition: Holding information active over a delay without manipulation, through volitional, attention-demanding processes; a core subprocess of working memory.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Cohen, Perlstein, Braver, Nystrom, Noll, Jonides & Smith (1997) *Nature* 386:604–608

processes.definition: Binding of multiple items into a single unit in memory to expand effective capacity.

recent_references.citation_string: Thalmann, Souza & Oberauer (2019) *JEP: Learning, Memory, and Cognition* 45:37–55

processes.definition: Transformation of information held in working memory (reordering, reversing, combining) beyond simple maintenance.

fundamental_references.citation_string: D'Esposito, Postle, Ballard & Lease (1999) *Brain and Cognition* 41:66–86

recent_references.citation_string: Nee, Brown, Askren, Berman, Demiralp, Krawitz & Jonides (2013) *Cerebral Cortex* 23:264–282

processes.definition: Covert repetition of material to refresh its representation in short-term memory, especially for verbal material (articulatory rehearsal).

fundamental_references.journal: Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior
fundamental_references.citation_string: Baddeley, Thomson & Buchanan (1975) *Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior* 14:575–589

recent_references.citation_string: Camos, Lagner & Barrouillet (2009) *Journal of Memory and Language* 61:457–469

processes.definition: Short-term storage of spatial locations and spatial relations.
Short-term storage of phonological/verbal information, historically "phonological loop"; supports digit span, Sternberg with letters.

recent_references.citation_string: Acheson, Hamidi, Binder & Postle (2011) *Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience* 23:1358–1367

processes.definition: Short-term storage and manipulation of visual information; capacity limited to ~3–4 items.
System for the short-term maintenance, updating, and manipulation of task-relevant information over delays of seconds.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Baddeley & Hitch (1974) in *Psychology of Learning and Motivation*

recent_references.citation_string: Oberauer, Lewandowsky, Awh, Brown, Conway et al. (2018) *Psychological Bulletin* 144:885–958

aliases.name: Updating
aliases.note: Pre-2026-04-17 name; renamed for specificity — plain 'Updating' was memory-context-underspecified.

processes.definition: Replacement or revision of information currently held active in working memory with newly task-relevant content. One of three core executive functions in the Miyake et al. unity/diversity framework; decomposable into retrieval, transformation, and substitution subcomponents (Ecker et al.); implemented by a cortico-striatal gating mechanism that opens to admit new content and closes to protect maintenance. Distinct from long-term-memory reconsolidation (which occurs when retrieval triggers a prediction error and targets stored representations rather than the focus of attention). Indexed behaviorally by N-Back, running-span, and keep-track performance.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Miyake, Friedman, Emerson, Witzki, Howerter & Wager (2000) *Cognitive Psychology* 41:49–100

recent_references.journal: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition
recent_references.citation_string: Ecker, Lewandowsky, Oberauer & Chee (2010) *Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition* 36:170–189
Frank, Loughry & O'Reilly (2001) *Cognitive, Affective, & Behavioral Neuroscience* 1:137–160
Wahlheim & Zacks (2025) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 29(4):380–392 — situates working-memory updating within the broader event-segmentation account of memory updating at event boundaries

processes.notes: Renamed 2026-04-17 from `hed_updating` (+ merged duplicate `hed_updating_wm`) for specificity — plain "Updating" is memory-context-underspecified.

processes.definition: Action in conflict with another agent's interests for a contested resource.
Coordinated action among agents for mutual benefit, often at individual cost.
Reproduction of observed actions or behaviors.
Differential processing of members of one's own group versus other groups.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Tajfel, Billig, Bundy & Flament (1971) *European Journal of Social Psychology* 1:149–178

processes.definition: Coordinated focus by two or more agents on the same object or event, involving shared awareness that attention is joint.

aliases.name: Mentalizing
aliases.note: Process verb; merged during reframe. More common in neuroimaging literature.

aliases.name: Theory of mind
aliases.note: Capacity/competence noun; the term used in developmental and clinical literatures.

processes.definition: Representation of another's viewpoint, visual or cognitive.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Piaget & Inhelder (1956) *The Child's Conception of Space*

tasks.canonical_name: Reading the Mind in the Eyes Task

processes.definition: Contingent positive or negative responses to another's prior behavior.
Discrimination between self-generated and other-generated states or actions.
Choice involving other agents and their preferences, often in strategic settings.
Visual perception of socially relevant stimuli, including faces, bodies, and actions.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Allison, Puce & McCarthy (2000) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 4:267–278

tasks.canonical_name: Reading the Mind in the Eyes Task

processes.definition: Attribution of attributes to individuals based on social category membership.
Imagined rotation of a 2D or 3D figure to compare with another figure; RT increases linearly with angular disparity.
Memory for the arrangement and location of objects and environments.

fundamental_references.journal: The Hippocampus as a Cognitive Map
fundamental_references.citation_string: O'Keefe & Nadel (1978) *The Hippocampus as a Cognitive Map*

processes.definition: The act of locking in a selected option once accumulated evidence or value crosses a decision boundary; measured by response-time distributions, drift-diffusion boundary parameters, and motor preparation signatures.

recent_references.citation_string: Gold JI & Shadlen MN (2007) *Annual Review of Neuroscience* 30:535-574
Ratcliff R, Smith PL, Brown SD & McKoon G (2016) *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* 20:260-281

processes.definition: Devaluation of a reward as a function of the delay to its receipt, typically hyperbolic.
Choice among options that differ in timing of outcomes; choice between smaller-sooner and larger-later.
Estimation of likelihoods of events, often deviating from normative Bayes.

recent_references.citation_string: Griffiths, Kemp & Tenenbaum (2008) in *Cambridge Handbook of Computational Psychology*

aliases.name: Risky decision making
aliases.note: Names the task class rather than the cognitive operation.

processes.definition: Neural and behavioral evaluation of outcome uncertainty.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Knutson, Adams, Fong & Hommer (2001) *Journal of Neuroscience* 21:RC159

processes.definition: Assignment of a subjective value to a prospective option, integrating reward magnitude, probability, delay, and effort; indexed by willingness-to-pay, subjective ratings, and value-coded neural signals in vmPFC and striatum.

fundamental_references.citation_string: Becker GM, DeGroot MH & Marschak J (1964) *Behavioral Science* 9:226-232

recent_references.citation_string: Bartra O, McGuire JT & Kable JW (2013) *NeuroImage* 76:412-427
Levy DJ & Glimcher PW (2012) *Current Opinion in Neurobiology* 22:1027-1038

processes.definition: Choice among options that differ in subjective value, computed across attributes.

recent_references.citation_string: Levy DJ & Glimcher PW (2012) *Current Opinion in Neurobiology* 22:1027–1038
